lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Hello there and welcome to the Natural History exhibit of the Varrock Museum!

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Hello. So what is it you do here?

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Well, we look after all of the research in this section.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: When I'm not doing that, I'm teaching people like yourself about how wonderful the natural world is.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Nice. So then, I've got a few questions for you.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Well, fire away and I'll give you an insight into the exciting world of animals.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Hello again, [sir/madam], how can I help you on this fine day?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: I was hoping you could tell me about something.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about lizards.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Ahh lizards, the scaly carnivores.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If you just follow me to the display case I shall explain all about them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Contrary to popular belief, even though most lizards have a yellow belly, they are not in fact cowardly. Most Gielinor lizards will not shy away from a good fight and can be very tough.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: This is due to a very thick and tough hide, made from thousands of thick scales. Most people who claim to be hunters have a very hard time trying to dispatch lizards.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: In fact, the only people to successfully discern how to kill these tough little squamatas are the five legendary Slayer masters, although we assume they must have some kind of natural predator.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Interestingly enough, these scales are made from the same substance that your hair is comprised of. This substance is called keratin.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Lizards have a very well developed sense of vision and hearing. Some people think that some lizards have a third eye!

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: A tiny, light-sensitive, transparent structure on top of the head that helps them regulate how long they stay in the sun.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: This is vital for the cold-blooded lizards who have no means to regulate their internal temperature.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Like many cold-blooded creatures, if they are subjected to a sudden decrease in temperature, they will become sluggish and sleepy.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: And this concludes my short lecture on lizards. I hope you've enjoyed yourselves.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about the tortoises.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Ahh tortoises, the armoured ancients.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If you just follow me to the display case I shall explain all about them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: The tortoise is a very well defended beast. It uses an armoured shell just like its aquatic cousins the terrapin and the turtle.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Tortoises can vary in size from about as long as your hand to as big as a unicorn. Most land tortoises eat nothing but plants in the wild.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Did you know you can tell how old a tortoise is by the number of rings in its shell, just like a tree.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Most land-based tortoises eat plants, feeding on grazing grasses, weeds, leafy greens, flowers, and cabbages.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Tortoises generally live as long as people, and some individual ones are known to have lived longer than 300 years.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Because of this, they symbolise longevity within some cultures, such as gnomes who also breed them for battle.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: The oldest tortoise ever recorded was Mibbiwocket, who was presented to the King Healthorg the Great, by the famous explorer Admiral Bake, shortly after its birth.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Mibbiwocket is still in the care of the gnomish royal family.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: And this concludes my short lecture on tortoises. I hope you've enjoyed yourselves.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about dragons.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Ahh dragons, the mighty hunters of the sky.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If you just follow me to the display case I shall explain all about them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: The dragons of Gielinor are a most confusing species. Standing at approximately twelve feet tall, these imposing predators strike fear into the hearts and minds of most sane folk.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: However, if you delve a little deeper into their history and lifestyle, a few things stick out as very unusual.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: With most species there is a line, an ancestry if you will, whereby you can track how a creature has come to be in its present form.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: For instance, you can trace the ancestry of the common house cat back to the same creature that became the sabre-toothed kyatt.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: However, with the dragon, no such root ancestor can be found.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: There are many forms of dragon, such as the common coloured and the metallic, or ferrous, dragon.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: They colonise many areas of Gielinor, though most notably, sites of ancient battles and small dank caves.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Eating habits tend to vary, with the majority of their food being meat. However, it has also been noted that they can consume metals just as easily, with runite being thought of as a delicacy.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Throughout history, dragons have appeared in myth and legend as fearsome adversaries and cunning creatures.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: However, modern evidence does not support this. Most young dragons are largely creatures of instinct with a strong vicious streak.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: The lifespan of the common dragon is as yet unknown, as no dragon has ever been observed dying of old age.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Although, it has been mooted that spontaneous combustion could be considered a natural cause of death for this species.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: And this concludes my short lecture on dragons. I hope you've enjoyed yourselves.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about the wyverns.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Ahh, wyverns. The extinct lizards.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If you just follow me to the display case I shall explain all about them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: The wyverns' tale is a sad one. This now extinct species is presumed in some way to be related to the dragon.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: In that they are both large flying reptiles. The wyvern stands much shorter than the adult dragon and only has two legs as opposed to the dragon's four.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Much of the evidence for wyvern behaviour comes from the reconstruction of old bones in the icy mountains of Asgarnia.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: One is that Asgarnia was at one time a much more temperate climate than it is now. The other is that wyverns could generate fire internally in much the same way as dragons.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If they follow the dragon paradigm, then they would have been carnivores, feeding on cows, sheep and other livestock animals.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: How and why the wyverns became extinct is something of a mystery. Though if you consider the theory that the climate of Asgarnia changed suddenly, then this could provide an explanation.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: There are some inconsistencies in the findings we have for the wyverns, such as the odd wear patterns of some of bones, which really could only have happened after the creature died.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Also the bones we have collected remain a little below room temperature wherever they are kept.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: They have also been shown to radiate a very weak magical aura.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: I'm sure that in due time, these mysteries will be solved.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: And this concludes my short lecture on wyverns. I hope you've enjoyed yourselves.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: That's enough education for one day.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Nonsense! There's always room for more.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: And remember, science isn't dull!

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Hello again, [sir/madam], how can I help you on this fine day?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: I was hoping you could tell me about something.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about snails.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Ahh snails, the gelatinous gastropods.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If you just follow me to the display case I shall explain all about them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Snails move like worms by squishing up and stretching out, very, very slowly. They also make a lot of slime, in order to aid moving by reducing friction.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: They also use slime for protection. For instance, snails can use their slime to crawl over razor-blades without being hurt. It also helps keep away dangerous insects like ants.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: When they hide in their shells, snails secrete a special type of slime, which dries to cover the entrance of their shells like a 'trapdoor'. This is called an operculum.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: The snails of Morytania are the most malignant molluscs ever to have been studied.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: They are broken down into two distinct species: achatina acidia and achatina acidia giganteus or, as they are more commonly known, the acid-spitting snail and the giant acid-spitting snail.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Both of these varieties are voracious carnivores, using their mutated mouthpieces to spit a glob of powerful acid to kill their foe.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: They then simply have to wait, whilst the digestive juices make short work of the poor creature. Then, they simply slurp up what remains.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: How these strange creatures came to be is still something of a mystery. The most prevalent theory suggests that they mutated, as a reaction to an 'as yet unknown' pollutant that has appeared in the swamps.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: The local populace has capitalised on the appearance of these strange species, using their shells to fashion a rudimentary helm that is fairly resistant to the snails acid.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Other known uses of snail by-products include a tasty local delicacy and a fireproof oil.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: And this concludes my short lecture on snails. I hope you've enjoyed yourselves.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about monkeys.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Ahh monkeys, the simian collective.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If you just follow me to the display case I shall explain all about them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: A monkey is a member of either of two known groupings of simian primates. These two known groupings are the Karamja monkeys and the 'harmless' monkeys.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Because of their similarity to monkeys, apes such as chimpanzees and gibbons are often called monkeys by accident.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Though some natural historians don't consider them to be monkeys. Also, a few monkey species have the word 'ape' in their common name.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: The Karamja monkeys are rumoured to be fairly cunning and intelligent creatures, although rumours that they have learned human speech is anecdotal at best.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: In appearance, they stand much shorter than a human and tend to move in a hunched fashion. Karamja monkeys also sport a red mohawk, though it is unknown whether this is an affectation or not.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: They are very fond of bananas and bitternuts, eating them in huge quantities whenever they can get their paws on them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: The harmless monkeys of Mos Le'Harmless are a very similar, but in some ways entirely different, breed. They stand roughly the same size but are a lighter colour.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Interestingly, Karamaja monkeys have a deep dislike of seaweed, though this may stem from the actions of a number of irresponsible people.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: And this concludes my short lecture on monkeys. I hope you've enjoyed yourselves.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about the sea slugs.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Ahh sea slugs, the cute crustaceans.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If you just follow me to the display case I shall explain all about them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: The term 'sea slug' is something of a misnomer. Whilst these small creatures have a soft body like that of a slug, they also possess a very hard shell like a snail.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Very little is actually known about the sea slug, as we have, as yet, been unable to procure a sample for observation and study.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: For some reason all expeditions we have sent have either vanished mysteriously, or those on the expedition have sent letters back announcing their desire to leave the Museum and go on to other things.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: It is presumed that the species is native to the very deep waters around the eastern Ardougne coastline.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: There must be some natural resource in the area that the sea slugs are using, as the underwater habitat there is much the same around many coastal areas on Gielinor.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Through looking at similar species we have determined that the sea slug is a harmless little creature. It spends much of its life grazing on seaweed and other plant life.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: There are reports that these reclusive animals have two large fangs at their front, though this is assumed to be either for decorative or defensive purposes.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If they do follow the same pattern as other similar creatures, the shell will be nigh on impervious to most attacks. The exposed soft skin may have a number of nematocysts, or stinging organs, similar to jellyfish.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: It is typical of prey animals such as these to develop some kind of unique defence mechanism that allows them to survive.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If only we could acquire one for study. I'm sure we would find this mechanism to be truly unique.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: And this concludes my short lecture on sea slugs. I hope you've enjoyed yourselves.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about snakes.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Ahh snakes, the slithering squamata.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If you just follow me to the display case I shall explain all about them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Serpentes, or ophidia, is the suborder under squamata that snakes belong to, like a big family.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Unlike lizards, snakes have no limbs whatsoever; not that this limits them. Snakes live in or near every habitat in the world.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Found in nice tropical forests, temperate lattitudes and even in the ocean - some have adapted specialised stomach juices that they inject into their prey as venom, while others prefer to grab and crush their food.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: While others still are fast hunters who use speed and strength to overcome their prey.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: The sense of smell in snakes has been enhanced in an amazing way. With most animals, like you and me, tiny particles are filtered through the nose.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: However, instead of using just their nose, these animals use their tongues as well. When a lizard or a snake wants to smell it's surroundings, it will wave its tongue around and pick up the particles in the air.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: The tongue then returns to the mouth and the tips of the tongue are pushed up against two tiny pits in the roof of the snake's mouth.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Since these pits are split apart from each other, the tongue itself also has to split. This is why snakes have forked tongues.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: So the next time you see a snake sticking it's tongue out at you, remember, it's sniffing the air, not trying to bite you.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: And this concludes my short lecture on snakes. I hope you've enjoyed yourselves.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Hello again, [sir/madam], how can I help you on this fine day?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: I was hoping you could tell me about something.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about natural history.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Well, the field of natural history covers a wide range of sciences.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: So we use biology, the study of living things, botany, the study of plants and zoology, the study of animals.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Though the field is growing all the time and we're also using techniques from magic, astrology and numerology.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: A person interested in natural history is known as a naturalist.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about the terrorbirds.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Ahh terrorbirds, the fastest bird on two legs.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If you just follow me to the display case I shall explain all about them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Terrorbirds live in nomadic groups of between five and fifty birds that often travel together with other grazing animals.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: They mainly feed on seeds and other plants. They also eat insects such as locusts if they can catch them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: They have no teeth to chew with, so they swallow pebbles that help to grind the swallowed foods in the gizzard.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: They can go without water for a long time, exclusively living off the water in the plants. However, they enjoy water and frequently take baths when they can.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Terrorbirds are known to eat almost anything, particularly in captivity, where opportunity is increased.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Terrorbirds usually weigh a little less than a small unicorn. The feathers of adult males are mostly green, with some white on the wings and tail.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: There are claws on two of the wings' fingers and their strong legs have no feathers. The bird stands on two toes, with the bigger one resembling a hoof. Its feet have no claws.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: This is an adaptation unique to terrorbirds that appears to aid in running. Their legs are powerful enough to kill even large animals.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: The gnomes in particular, prize the terrorbird for its fast running speed, using them as mounts whenever possible.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: There are a number of recorded incidents of people being attacked and killed. Big males can be very territorial and aggressive, and can attack and kick very powerfully with their legs.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: A terrorbird is so fast, it can easily outrun any human athlete.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: And this concludes my short lecture on terrorbirds. I hope you've enjoyed yourselves.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about the Kalphite Queen.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Ahh kalphites, the insectoid eating machines.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If you just follow me to the display case I shall explain all about them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: The kalphites, otherwise known as the kalphiscarabeinae, are perhaps the largest species of insect on the face of Gielinor. Their queen is called kalphiscarabeinae pasha.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Most of the early documentation and research on this fearsome predatory species was performed by the noted bug hunter Iqbar Ali-Abdula.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: This, of course, was before he was driven insane by his research and ran off into the desert, screaming.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Kalphites are related to beetles and scorpions; they are mainly green in colour. Some have remarkable antennae which can detect the slightest movement. Their carapace is composed of armoured plates called lamellae.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: This shell can be compressed into a ball or fanned out like leaves, in order to sense odours. The front legs are adapted for digging the enormous tunnel systems that serve as their nests.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: They exist in a caste-based society, with the soft shelled larvae at the bottom, up through the workers, soldiers and finally the queen.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Voracious carnivores, a pack of adult workers can strip the flesh from a full grown camel in a matter of seconds, leaving nothing but a few bones and strips of fur for other scavengers to pick over.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: They typically live in large nests marked by the rock hard pillars found in hot, arid deserts, such as the one south-west of Al Kharid, which rise out of the sands like the tombs of desert pharaohs.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Indeed, there is some relationship between the Kalphite Queen and the desert god Scabaras, but no one is really sure what.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: During the early part of the fourth age, Scabaras proclaimed himself omnipotent and outlawed worship of all other gods save him.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: When the people eventually revolted against his repressive rule and banished Scabaras, it is said his blood washed over the scarabs and transformed them into the kalphites we know today.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Of course, any right-minded scientist discounts these myths as mere stories, with no historical basis in fact.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: And this concludes my short lecture on kalphites. I hope you've enjoyed yourselves.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Hello again, [sir/madam], how can I help you on this fine day?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: I was hoping you could tell me about something.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about camels.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Ahh camels, the ships of the desert.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If you just follow me to the display case I shall explain all about them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: The term camel refers to either of the two known species of camelid.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: There is the bactrian, which has two humps and can be found throughout Al Kharid. It also refers to the Ugthanki, or one-humped camel.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Camels are ill-tempered beasts at the best of times, requiring a great deal of time and effort to tame.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: This would explain why used camel salesmen are in such a hurry to be rid of them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: In the wild, camels have been known to lie in wait and ambush hapless travellers, before devouring them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: This is quite surprising, as most domestic camels are happy eating nothing but vegetables.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Camel milk is much more nutritious than cow milk and goes well in the strong desert drink akin to tea.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Another useful camel by-product is dung. Their dung is very dry, due to the highly efficient metabolism of the camel.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Scientific research has also shown that chilli has a disastrous effect on a camel's digestive system, which produces toxic dung.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: And this concludes my short lecture on camels. I hope you've enjoyed yourselves.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about leeches.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Ahh leeches, the haemophagic parasites.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If you just follow me to the display case I shall explain all about them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Leeches are fascinating creatures and are very similar to worms in most respects.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: They like to inhabit streams, rivers and seas, but their preference is for stagnant pools of water.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: One of the most common misconceptions is that all leeches feed on blood.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Most leeches are meat eaters, feeding on a variety of invertebrates such as worms, snails, insect larvae and snails.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Those that do feed on blood have developed an amazing method of doing so. Firstly they latch onto the skin using a ring of tiny teeth, before injecting their prey with an anaesthetic.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Then they bite into the skin using a Y-shaped mouthpiece and introducing a chemical that stops the blood from clotting.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: They will then feed until they are completely full, sometimes doubling in size!

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Most leeches are very small, measuring no more than the length of your middle finger. An exception to these are the leeches of Morytania, which can reach the size of a dog.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: They are much more mobile than their smaller cousins and are able to jump rather high when attacking.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Quite how these leeches came to be so big is something of a mystery.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: All we can assume is that there is some kind of environmental influence, which has governed their immense growth.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: And this concludes my short lecture on leeches. I hope you've enjoyed yourselves.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about moles.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Ahh moles, the mammalian mountain makers.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If you just follow me to the display case I shall explain all about them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Now, moles are small mammals of the talpidae family. These subterranean burrowers mainly live on a diet of slugs, snails and insects.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: They vary greatly in habitat and can be found in almost every part of Gielinor. Some species have even known to be aquatic!

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Male moles are known as boars with the females called sows. Should you come across a group of moles, you would call them a labour.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Moles are considered to be an agricultural pest in most places, digging up the ground and leaving molehills all over the place.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: This has been highlighted in Falador by Wyson the Gardener who, after using some Malignus Mortifer's Super Ultra Flora Growth Potion, managed to create Gielinor's only known species of giant mole.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: This fearsome beast has huge claws, wicked teeth and a penchant for shiny objects. It is a very tough animal with a thick protective hide and an ill temperament.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: That said, they do benefit the soil by aerating and tilling it, adding to its fertility. Contrary to popular belief, moles don't eat plant roots.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: And this concludes my short lecture on moles. I hope you've enjoyed yourselves.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about penguins.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Ahh penguins, the cunning birds of the sea.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If you just follow me to the display case I shall explain all about them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: This often-maligned aquatic bird is known to be native to the ice fields of Etceteria, although they have been known to live as far as the fields of Lumbridge.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: These aquatic birds seem to spend the majority of their time eating and fostering their young.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: For creatures with such small brains, they do seem to have a disproportionate capacity for forward thinking and planning. As this serves no natural purpose, scholars are divided as to how this evolved.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Their diet consists mainly of fish, squid and a small shrimp-like creature called krill. However, some have developed a taste for the mushrooms that grow around fairy rings.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Penguins primarily rely on their vision while hunting. What we don't know is how penguins locate prey in the darkness, or at great depths.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Some theories suggest that penguins are helped by some sort of extra sensory perception; perhaps even precognition.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Penguins spend a long time going without food when they are breeding. In fact, they won't even leave their nests if they can help it.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Fortunately, most penguins build up a layer of fat to keep them warm and provide energy until the moult is over.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: And this concludes my short lecture on penguins. I hope you've enjoyed yourselves.

sections.title: Attempting to bring a follower to one of the lectures

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: If you just follow me to the display case I shall explain all about them.

lines.speaker: Natural historian
lines.text: Hmm, I'm afraid I don't allow pets in my lectures, the demonstrations can scare them.
